title: LIBERATE Act
plain_summary: The LIBERATE Act would create a special group of 18 people to look at government rules that make it hard for American businesses to operate. This group would include government workers and business experts who would study these rules carefully. They would have to write reports for Congress on a regular basis about what they found. Each year, they would tell Congress which rules should be changed or gotten rid of to help businesses work better.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Locating the Inefficiencies of Bureaucratic Edicts to Reform And Transform the Economy Act or the LIBERATE Act for short.

sections.title: Section 2 Regulatory Oversight and Review Task Force
sections.text: This section creates a group called the Regulatory Oversight and Review Task Force. This group will look at government rules and decide which ones should be changed or removed. The group will have eighteen members total. The Director of the Office of Management and Budget will lead the group. One person will come from the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs. Sixteen people will come from private businesses outside the government. The Senate majority leader, Senate minority leader, House Speaker, and House minority leader will each choose four business people to join the group. Each business person must know about government rules, following government rules, economics, law, or running a business. At least two people chosen by each leader must represent small businesses. No more than two people chosen by each leader can belong to the same political party. The President must choose all the business members within thirty days after this law passes. The group must work with the Government Accountability Office. Group members will not get paid for this work. The group will look at government rules that hurt American businesses in several ways. These include rules that stop fair competition and make American companies less able to compete with foreign companies. The group will also look at rules that make it hard for new businesses to start, increase the costs of making things in America, create expensive requirements that businesses must follow, make it hard to get permits, hurt American energy production and make America depend on other countries for energy, limit mining including mining of important minerals, or make it harder for people to invest money in the American economy. The group must create a website where people can send in their ideas about which rules should be changed. Government agencies must give the group any documents and information the group asks for to help with this work. Within fifteen days after the group first meets, they must start asking for written suggestions from the public, interested groups, government agencies, and other relevant organizations about the rules they are studying. People can send suggestions through the group's website, regulations.gov, regular mail, or other written ways. The group must publish every suggestion they receive in the Federal Register, on their website, and on regulations.gov. The group must also hold public meetings in different parts of the country to get more feedback from people about these rules. The group must review all the information they receive and consider including it in their reports. The group must send reports to Congress every three months and every year about what they have found. Each report must analyze the government rules they identified and give recommendations for changing, combining, making consistent, or eliminating these rules. Every year by the first day both houses of Congress are meeting after May first, the Director of the Office of Management and Budget must send Congress a special message on behalf of the group. This message must list each rule the group thinks should be eliminated and explain why each rule should be eliminated. The message must be delivered to the Clerk of the House of Representatives and the Secretary of the Senate and printed in the Congressional Record. Congress can then pass a joint resolution to eliminate some or all of the rules recommended by the group. The law sets up specific procedures for how Congress must handle these joint resolutions, including time limits for committee review and floor debate in both the House and Senate. No extra money is authorized to pay for this group. The group must use money already available to the Office of Management and Budget.
